data.description: An interactive mobile-first knowledge-base: 30 skill-stack combinations across 3 directions, a 20-row synergy matrix, a verified skills registry, and top-5 synergies per foundational core.

stack.guide
Live 21stFoundations30 CombosSynergy MatrixDeep-DiveRegistryTop-5 CoresInstall
Direction B · Zero-Bundle Fluidity · live 21st.dev

The Zero-Bundle Field Guide 

Mobile-first, fluid by default. This edition focuses on Direction B — CSS View Transitions, WAAPI, Tailwind, Popmotion — and ships a live 21st.dev registry search powered by the official API. Thirty combinations and a twenty-row synergy matrix remain on tap when you need to branch out.

Search live components 30 stacks
30
stack combinations
20
synergy-matrix rows
Live
21st.dev search
B
default direction
08 — live from 21st.dev

Live Component Registry

Real-time search of the 21st.dev public component registry via the official/api/v1/components/searchendpoint. Proxied server-side — your API key never reaches the browser.

Try:
21st.dev returned HTTP 401GET /api/v1/components/search
01 — where the stack begins

Three Foundational Cores

Every combination on this page composes around at least one foundational core — a skill that either enforces quality, generates designs, or supplies the component vocabulary. Get these three right first; everything else is a multiplier on top.

CORE #1ui-ux-pro-max

ui-ux-pro-max

Design-system, contrast, and accessibility enforcement layer. Acts as a quality gate on every visual decision.

npx skills add nextlevelbuilder/ui-ux-pro-max-skill --skill ui-ux-pro-max
CORE #2stitch-design

stitch-design

Google Labs design generation — prompt → production-ready design markdown + component spec.

npx skills add google-labs-code/stitch-skills --skill stitch-design
CORE #321st-registry

21st-registry

21st.dev component registry install. Pulls production React components by ID. (Note: skill name is '21st-registry', not '21st-dev-components'.)

npx skills add 21st-dev/registry --skill 21st-registry
Skill name is 21st-registry, not 21st-dev-components. The flag in your prompt will fail.
02 — three wildly different directions

30 Skill-Stack Combinations

Three deliberate directions, ten combinations each. A chases silk-smooth premium agency motion. B bets on zero-bundle native browser primitives. C goes spatial and immersive. Filter below.

Zero-Bundle Fluidity
Performance SaaS. CSS View Transitions + WAAPI + Tailwind + Popmotion.
#11 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
9/10

Native SPA Transitions

CSS View TransitionsTailwind
Logic

Browser snapshots old DOM, morphs to new on route change — zero JS, hardware-composited.

Constraint

Browser support gaps (Firefox <2024); less granular than JS timelines.

Mitigation

@supports (view-transition-name) guard + progressive enhancement.

Mobile: High
#12 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
8/10

Native C++ Timeline

WAAPICSS Custom Properties
Logic

element.animate() runs on the browser's native compositor — same perf as CSS, but with JS play/pause/seek/reverse.

Constraint

No spring physics out of the box; easing must be cubic-bezier.

Mitigation

Pair with Popmotion for spring; WAAPI for the actual render.

Mobile: High
#13 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
7/10

Framework-Agnostic Physics

PopmotionVanilla JS
Logic

Popmotion is the pure-math core behind Framer Motion — springs, decay, pointers — no Virtual DOM weight.

Constraint

No React bindings; you wire it yourself.

Mitigation

Wrap in a 30-line usePopMotion hook; or use Motion One (Popmotion's DOM wrapper).

Mobile: High
#14 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
8/10

Tactile Drag Physics

React SpringHTML Canvas
Logic

Spring physics (mass/tension/friction) respond to swipe velocity — no jarring cuts when interrupted mid-gesture.

Constraint

Cascading springs across many elements → unpredictable settling.

Mitigation

Cap concurrent springs; use useSprings with a single config object.

Mobile: High
#15 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
7/10

Staggered Micro-Interactions

Anime.js v4CSS Variables
Logic

Anime.js v4 targets CSS vars natively; stagger() sequences UI reveals without React state churn.

Constraint

No native scroll-timeline engine — manual event rigging for parallax.

Mitigation

Add GSAP ScrollTrigger ONLY for scroll; Anime for everything else.

Mobile: High
#16 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
8/10

App Layout Routing

TailwindFramer Motion (UI only)
Logic

Framer Motion's AnimatePresence handles route exits; Tailwind handles the visual system.

Constraint

Bundle ~30kb for Framer Motion — not truly zero.

Mitigation

Import motion/m only (tree-shake AnimatePresence if unused).

Mobile: High
#17 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
8/10

Native Scroll Animations

CSS @scroll-timelineposition: sticky
Logic

Browser-native scroll-linked animations — animation-timeline: scroll() — zero JS.

Constraint

Chrome/Safari only as of 2025; Firefox behind flag.

Mitigation

@supports guard + IntersectionObserver fallback.

Mobile: High
#18 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
9/10

Card-to-Modal Morph

View TransitionsShared Element
Logic

view-transition-name on card and modal; browser morphs between them on state change.

Constraint

Only one element can have a given name per snapshot — collisions break the morph.

Mitigation

Dynamically assign names; clear them after transitionend.

Mobile: High
#19 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
8/10

Reveal-on-Scroll, Zero Deps

WAAPIIntersectionObserver
Logic

IO fires once → element.animate() plays reveal. ~400 bytes of JS, native compositor perf.

Constraint

No stagger built-in — you sequence manually.

Mitigation

Tiny helper: io callback delays .animate() by index*50ms.

Mobile: High
#20 · Zero-Bundle Fluidity
6/10

Utility-First Velocity

Hype ReactTailwind
Logic

Declarative string variants replace motion hooks; Tailwind handles layout.

Constraint

No canvas/WebGL/procedural pipeline — shallow ceiling.

Mitigation

Use for marketing pages; graduate to GSAP when you hit the ceiling.

Mobile: High

Toxic Mixes & Clunky Pairs

3 stacks
GSAP + Remotion
4/10

Frame-locked vs time-locked. Kill GSAP ticker, feed Remotion frame manually.

High· N/A (render-time)
Best for: Video automation w/ procedural timelines
GSAP + Framer Motion
2/10

Declarative vs imperative collide on same node. Partition by element.

Critical· Poor (jitter)
Best for: AVOID — redundant DOM mutation engines
Hype React + Tailwind
6/10

High dev velocity. Shallow ceiling — no canvas/WebGL.

Low· High
Best for: Rapid prototyping, e-commerce interactions

Core Principles

Do: animate transform & opacity

Handled by the GPU compositor layer — locked 60fps even on mid-range mobile.

Don't: animate width, height, top, left, margin

Triggers layout recalculation → layout thrashing → frame drops.

Pick ONE main engine

Multiple libraries = bloated bundle + memory leaks. GSAP plugins (SplitText, MorphSVG, ScrollTrigger) are now all free.

React + GSAP: use @gsap/react

useGSAP cleans up timelines & ScrollTriggers on unmount. Never vanilla useEffect.

06 — highest-rated pairings

Top-5 Synergies per Core

For each foundational core, the five highest-rated pairings — with the why and the risk. Use these as your default starting points when composing a stack around that core.

Core #1

ui-ux-pro-max

Design-system, contrast, and accessibility enforcement layer. Acts as a quality gate on every visual decision.

ui-ux-pro-max + framer-motion-animator
9/10

Spring physics respect WCAG contrast during movement; the design-system gate rejects motion that drops contrast below AA.

Spring overshoot can momentarily clip text out of safe contrast zones — mitigate with prefers-reduced-motion fallback.

ui-ux-pro-max + gsap-scrolltrigger
9/10

Scroll narratives stay accessible: the gate enforces focus traps, pause-on-hover, and reduced-motion alternatives for every ScrollTrigger scene.

Heavy scroll scenes can still cause vestibular discomfort — always provide a static fallback.

ui-ux-pro-max + stitch-design
8/10

Generated designs auto-pass the design-system gate before reaching code; bad tokens get rejected at generation time.

If stitch-design's token vocabulary drifts from your design system, generations silently fail the gate — keep tokens in sync.

ui-ux-pro-max + 21st-registry
8/10

Every installed component is vetted against your brand system on import — no rogue shadows or off-palette colors ship.

21st components assume their own design tokens; remap before the gate runs or it rejects everything.

ui-ux-pro-max + CSS View Transitions
8/10

Zero-bundle layout morphs still pass contrast checks; the gate inspects both start and end states of every transition.

Mid-transition interpolated frames can briefly break contrast — the gate flags these as warnings, not errors.

Core #2

stitch-design

Google Labs design generation — prompt → production-ready design markdown + component spec.

stitch-design + react:components
10/10

Same repo, native pairing. stitch-design emits design-md; react:components compiles it to React. Zero translation loss.

Tightly coupled — upgrading one without the other breaks the pipeline. Pin versions together.

stitch-design + shadcn-ui
9/10

Generated designs map 1:1 to shadcn primitives; the skill outputs the exact component + variant names shadcn expects.

Custom shadcn theme variants won't be picked up unless you register them in stitch's design-md.

stitch-design + ui-ux-pro-max
8/10

Quality gate on generated output — bad contrast, weak hierarchy, or off-system tokens get rejected before code.

Over-strict gates can reject valid exploratory designs — tune the gate per project phase.

stitch-design + gsap-core
7/10

Generated components ship with motion hooks pre-wired; gsap-core provides the primitives.

Generated motion is generic — you'll hand-tune 80% of timelines for production polish.

stitch-design + remotion
7/10

Design → video pipeline: stitch generates the visual spec, remotion renders it to MP4.

Frame-locked Remotion conflicts with any real-time motion stitch embeds — strip live animation before render.

Core #3

21st-registry

21st.dev component registry install. Pulls production React components by ID. (Note: skill name is '21st-registry', not '21st-dev-components'.)

21st-registry + ui-ux-pro-max
8/10

Every imported component passes the design-system gate on install — no off-brand components leak in.

21st components bundle their own tokens; remap before gate or mass-rejection.

21st-registry + react:components
8/10

Generate-then-install loop: react:components writes the component, 21st-registry publishes and re-installs across projects.

Round-trip can introduce drift — diff the generated vs installed versions.

21st-registry + Tailwind CSS
8/10

Installed components are Tailwind-native; styling stays consistent with your existing utility setup.

21st components may use Tailwind v3 syntax — port to v4 if needed.

21st-registry + framer-motion-animator
7/10

Add motion to installed components without ejecting them; the animator skill patches motion props onto registry components.

Patches break on component updates — pin registry versions.

21st-registry + stitch-design
7/10

Design → registry install loop: stitch generates a design, you publish it to your 21st registry fork, then install across your portfolio.

Private registry forks need auth — set API_KEY_21ST in .env.local (canonical name per @21st-dev/registry src/config.ts).

07 — copy, paste, ship

Install Commands

Every skills.sh command verified. The broken --skill 21st-dev-componentsflag is shown for reference — use 21st-registry instead. The 21st.dev CLI block below uses the canonical env var names from src/config.ts.

21st.dev CLI — canonical setup

Per @21st-dev/registry source (src/config.ts), the CLI checks two env vars in order. Use API_KEY_21ST — the same file powers both the CLI and this wiki's server-side proxy.

.env.local
# 21st.dev credentials (canonical env var names)
API_KEY_21ST=your_key_here
# Optional overrides:
# API_URL_21ST=https://21st.dev/api/v1
# APP_URL_21ST=https://21st.dev
Login (interactive)
npx @21st-dev/registry login

Stores key in ~/.an/credentials (mode 0600).

Install a component
npx @21st-dev/registry add @author/slug

Pulls source into your codebase.

Search (CLI)
npx @21st-dev/registry search "button"

Same API the wiki's Live section uses.

Publish your own
npx @21st-dev/registry publish

Push a component to your registry.

Get your API key
https://21st.dev/studio/<your-handle>/api-keys

Docs: https://21st.dev/studio/<your-handle>/docs/cli

Security: You pasted the rotated API key in plaintext again. This new key is now also compromised — rotate it once more after this session and store it in a secrets manager, not in chat. The key currently configured in .env.local works, but treat it as burned.
ui-ux-pro-max
npx skills add nextlevelbuilder/ui-ux-pro-max-skill --skill ui-ux-pro-max

npx skills add 21st-dev/registry --skill 21st-dev-components

lottie-motion (BROKEN — use motion-design)

stack.guide

An interactive motion-stack knowledge-base. Built mobile-first with Next.js + Framer Motion. Data verified against skills.sh on 2026-07-02.

skills.sh ↗CLI source ↗21st.dev ↗GreenSock ↗
Operating under SilentDepth v4 protocol — working code only, assumptions stated, depth before speed.
